title: Measurement of renal clearance, renal blood flow and GFR
body_html:
First remember a few key concepts and definitions:
  • renal blood flow (RBF) accounts for 25% of cardiac output
  • glomerular filtration rate (GFR) is the volume of fluid filtered from the glomerular capillaries into the Bowman's capsule per unit time. GFR is a key measure of renal function in clinical practice
  • renal clearance is the volume of plasma cleared of a substance per unit time

Image sourced from Wikipedia

Renal clearance

C = UV / P

C = clearance
U = urine concentration
V = urine volume/time (urine flow rate)
P = plasma concentration

Glomerular filtration rate

The glomerular filtration rate (GFR) can be calculated using the above clearance equation for inulin. Inulin is filtered but not reabsorbed or secreted.

Therefore, GFR = Uinulin * V / Pinulin

In clinical practice GFR is often estimated using the creatinine clearance as a proxy. Formulas such as the Modification of Diet in Renal Disease (MDRD) formula use creatinine and age to calculate an estimated GFR (eGFR).

Renal plasma flow

Again, the renal plasma flow (RPF) can be calculated using the clearance equation. Para-aminohippuric acid (PAH) is used as it both filtered and secreted.

RPF = CPAH = UPAH * V / PPAH

This is more correctly the estimated RPF as it does not measure plasma flow to parts of the kidney that do not filter and secrete PAH. The true value is around 10% higher.

Renal blood flow

Renal blood flow (RBF) is affected by a number of facts including:
  • vasoconstriction of renal arterioles (e.g. sympathetic nerve stimulation) → ↓ RBF

RBF = RPF / (1 - Haematocrit)

Filtration fraction

Filtration fraction (FF) is the proportion of the fluid reaching the kidneys which passes into the renal tubules. Is normally around 0.2 or 20%.

FF = GFR / RPF

Clinical correlation

ScenarioEffect on GFREffect on RPFEffect on FF
Constriction of afferent arterioles (e.g. sympathetic nerve stimulation, NSAIDs)
↓
↓
No change
Vasodilation of afferent arterioles (e.g. prostaglandins)
↑
↑
No change
Constriction of efferent arterioles (e.g. angiotensin II)
↑
↓
↑
Vasodilation of efferent arterioles (e.g. ACE inhibitors)
↓
↑
↓
Ureteric obstruction (e.g. ureteric stone)
↓
No change
↓
Decreased plasma protein concentration
↑
No change
↑

Reabsorption and secretion

Filtered load = GFR * plasma concentration
Excretion rate = V * urine concentration

Reabsorption rate = Filtered load - excretion rate
Secretion rate = Excretion rate - filtered load
2
